skill.name: pdf-official
skill.description: Guides PDF processing with Python libraries and command-line tools for text extraction, table extraction, PDF creation, page operations, images, encryption, and form filling.
skill.summary: Process, inspect, create, merge, split, and fill PDF documents with local tools.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk indicators, but review showed they are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, PDF examples, and documentation links. The remaining real risk is moderate: this community skill asks the assistant to run local PDF commands and scripts that read and write user-selected files.

medium_findings.title: Local File Writes From User-Supplied Paths
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts read user-provided PDFs and JSON files, then write generated PDFs, images, or field metadata to user-provided output paths. This is expected for PDF processing, but it can overwrite local files if paths are chosen carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are directly present in helper scripts and use paths supplied through command-line arguments. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but it is a real local filesystem risk.

medium_findings.title: External PDF Tool Commands Are Recommended
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to run local PDF tools such as pdftotext, qpdf, pdftk, pdfimages, and Python helper scripts. These commands are relevant to PDF processing, but they should be run only on trusted paths and reviewed inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and aligned with the skill purpose. I found no code that dynamically builds and executes shell commands, so the concern is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Malware Indicators Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The high C2 and weak cryptography alerts were triggered by PDF examples, coordinates, field values, password-protection examples, and ordinary code snippets. I found no evidence of command-and-control behavior, malicious cryptography, or hidden execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible documentation or normal PDF manipulation examples. No suspicious network endpoint, obfuscation, persistence, credential theft, or prompt injection text was found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to license terms, PDF standards, a form-handling article, and a pypdf issue. I found no code that sends PDF content, field data, secrets, or telemetry to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each URL is in a license text or source comment, and there are no HTTP client imports or request calls in the scripts reviewed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Writes From User-Supplied Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper scripts read user-provided PDFs and JSON files, then write generated PDFs, images, or field metadata to user-provided output paths. This is expected for PDF processing, but it can overwrite local files if paths are chosen carelessly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are directly present in helper scripts and use paths supplied through command-line arguments. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but it is a real local filesystem risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External PDF Tool Commands Are Recommended
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation instructs users to run local PDF tools such as pdftotext, qpdf, pdftk, pdfimages, and Python helper scripts. These commands are relevant to PDF processing, but they should be run only on trusted paths and reviewed inputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and aligned with the skill purpose. I found no code that dynamically builds and executes shell commands, so the concern is operational rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: PDF workflows often require several libraries and command-line tools. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical steps for extraction, conversion, form filling, and document assembly.
actual_capabilities: Extract text and tables from PDFs with pypdf, pdfplumber, and related examples.
Create new PDFs with reportlab and modify existing PDFs with pypdf or pdf-lib.
Merge, split, rotate, crop, watermark, encrypt, and decrypt PDF documents.
Convert PDF pages to images for OCR, visual inspection, and form analysis.
Detect fillable form fields and produce field metadata for structured form completion.
Fill non-fillable forms by validating bounding boxes and adding text annotations.
limitations: Requires local Python packages or command-line PDF utilities to be installed before use.
Scanned PDFs need OCR tools, and OCR quality depends on image clarity.
Non-fillable forms require careful visual inspection and bounding box validation.
PDF viewer behavior can vary, especially for form appearances and annotations.

use_cases.title: Prepare completed forms
use_cases.description: Identify PDF form fields, map user data to fields, and create a filled copy for review.

use_cases.title: Extract document data
use_cases.description: Pull text, tables, and metadata from reports, statements, and multi-page PDF files.

use_cases.title: Assemble document packets
use_cases.description: Merge, split, rotate, watermark, and optimize PDFs for organized document delivery.

prompt_templates.title: Extract PDF text
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the text from this PDF, preserve page breaks, and summarize any pages that appear blank or unreadable.

prompt_templates.title: Merge and organize files
prompt_templates.prompt: Combine these PDF files in order, rotate pages that need correction, and save a clean merged copy.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a PDF form
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether this PDF has fillable fields, identify each field, and create a completed PDF using the provided answers.

prompt_templates.title: Build an extraction workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a local workflow to extract tables from these PDFs, handle failures, and produce reviewable output files.

output_examples.input: A user asks to extract tables from a monthly PDF report.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the best extraction library, explains any table quality issues, and provides the extracted rows in a readable format.

output_examples.input: A user provides a non-fillable form and answers for each field.
output_examples.output: The assistant converts pages to images, validates entry areas, and creates a filled PDF with text annotations.

output_examples.input: A user needs several PDFs merged, split, and rotated.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends local commands or Python steps and produces an organized output document plan.

best_practices: Work on copies of PDFs and choose explicit output paths to avoid overwriting originals.
Inspect generated PDFs visually before submitting forms or sending documents externally.
Install only the PDF libraries and command-line tools needed for the current workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not run PDF commands on untrusted paths without checking the exact command first.
Do not assume OCR text is accurate without reviewing low-quality scans.
Do not skip validation images when filling non-fillable forms with annotations.

faq.question: Can this skill fill PDF forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports fillable fields and annotation-based filling for non-fillable forms.

faq.question: Does it work with scanned PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes, but scanned PDFs require image conversion and OCR tools for text extraction.

faq.question: Does the skill send PDFs to an external service?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed scripts process local files and do not make network requests.

faq.question: What tools may need installation?
faq.answer: Common tools include pypdf, pdfplumber, reportlab, pdf2image, pytesseract, qpdf, and poppler utilities.

faq.question: Can it extract tables?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance uses pdfplumber and related techniques for structured table extraction.

faq.question: What is the main safety concern?
faq.answer: The scripts and commands write local files, so users should review paths and keep backup copies.
